notes: HP's Unix HA clustering software. No NT version existed at time of study. Continues today as HPE Serviceguard for HP-UX and Linux.
Microsoft's enterprise OS platform. Report noted limitations in HA capabilities versus Unix. Evolved into Windows 2000 and later Windows Server editions.
HP's Unix variant. Primary platform for HP's HA solutions at time of study. Still supported by HPE but declining in market share.
HAO remote monitoring service. Identified as one of three competitive differentiators for HP's HA positioning.
Standard availability target equating to ~5.26 minutes downtime per year. Report recommended moving away from this 'techie-term' toward broader messaging.
Failover and load-balancing clustering technology. Central to HA strategies of the era. Evolved into modern cloud HA architectures and Kubernetes.
Marathon's software-based fault tolerance for Windows NT. Aberdeen recommended HP acquire Marathon for this capability.

Concept of HA spanning entire application stack rather than individual boxes. Aberdeen rated HP's position as defendable with 12-18 month window. Now mainstream in cloud-native resilience engineering.
Online Transaction Processing. Report noted HP's HA marketing was overly focused on Unix OLTP and should broaden to include email, data warehouses, e-business, supply chain, and call centers.
Referenced as the hardware platform for NT. Report noted HP must clearly communicate HA limits on Intel/NT in 1999-2000.
Tandem's fault-tolerant computing platform. Division of Compaq at time of study. Continues as HPE NonStop.
